scenario_summary: The user opposes ASTP/ONC's proposed removal of AI model card and transparency requirements from the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion. They believe transparency is essential for patient safety, trust in AI tools, and informed procurement decisions, and want to mobilize stakeholders (hospitals, clinical informatics organizations, patient safety advocates, cybersecurity experts, and academics) to retain these safeguards.
objective: Retain the current Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion requiring AI model cards and transparency disclosures (intended use, target population, known risks, training data characteristics, external validation) to ensure patient safety, informed clinical decision-making, and accountability in AI-assisted healthcare.

argument_angles.angle: Removing transparency requirements creates unacceptable patient safety risks when AI systems make or influence high-stakes clinical recommendations without disclosed performance data or known limitations.

Healthcare providers and patients have a fundamental right to know how AI systems were designed, tested, and validated before deploying them in clinical settings; removing model cards eliminates accountability.

Hospital and health system procurement teams need transparent AI model information to make evidence-based decisions about which tools to adopt and how to implement them safely in their local contexts.

The 'black box' problem is a documented barrier to AI adoption in healthcare; model cards directly address clinician and organizational concerns about trustworthiness and are a proven mechanism to build confidence.

Removing DSI transparency requirements creates a dangerous regulatory gap where AI-based clinical decision support tools face fewer disclosure obligations than traditional medical devices, undermining consistent safety standards.

The agency's claim that 'no publicly available evidence' supports transparency requirements conflates absence of evidence with evidence of absence; lack of published studies does not mean the criterion provides no value.

notes: This campaign strategically amplifies advocacy and patient-focused voices while maintaining credible industry and academic representation. Advocacy groups are boosted from 18.9% to 32% because they are most effective at framing this as a patient safety and transparency rights issue, and they have institutional credibility with regulators. Individual consumers (high + medium sophistication) are increased from 31.2% to 34% to emphasize the personal stakes and right-to-know framing. Academic voices are held at 8% (up from 1.7%) to provide evidence-based counterarguments to the agency's 'no evidence' claim. Industry-high is maintained at 17% (down from 30%) because while hospitals and health systems are natural allies, over-representing industry could undermine the patient-safety-first framing and invite accusations of vendor self-interest. The affinity_boost of 3.5 is set high because argument-voice alignment is critical: patient safety and accountability arguments must come from advocacy and consumer voices; procurement and trust-adoption arguments work best from industry and academic voices; the regulatory gap and evidence-absence arguments require academic rigor. Few DSI comments currently exist in the docket (the rule is new), so this campaign must establish the narrative that transparency requirements are preventive safeguards against future AI risks, not solutions to current problems.
